questions.text: How would you describe your current understanding of what artificial intelligence is and how it works?

options.text: I have little to no understanding of AI
I know the basics — AI uses data and algorithms
I can explain how LLMs generate text and understand their limitations

questions.text: When a large language model (like ChatGPT) generates a response, what is it actually doing?

options.text: Searching the internet for the answer
Retrieving information from a database of facts
Predicting the most likely next word based on patterns in training data
I'm not sure

questions.text: What does the term 'hallucination' mean in the context of AI?

options.text: When AI becomes self-aware
When AI generates confident-sounding but incorrect information
When AI refuses to answer a question
I haven't heard this term

questions.text: A colleague tells you: 'AI understands what students need and can replace tutoring.' How would you respond?

options.text: That sounds about right — AI is very advanced now
AI can help, but it doesn't truly understand — it processes patterns
AI simulates understanding through statistical prediction, which is useful but fundamentally different from human comprehension

questions.text: How familiar are you with the concept of multimodal AI (AI that processes images, audio, and text together)?

options.text: I haven't heard of this
I know it exists but haven't used it
I've used multimodal features (e.g., uploading images to ChatGPT) and understand the concept

feedback.beginner: You're at the beginning of your AI journey. Start with understanding what AI is and how language models work — this foundation will help everything else make sense.
feedback.intermediate: You have a good grasp of AI basics. Dive deeper into multimodal AI and the different layers of AI capability to round out your understanding.
feedback.advanced: Excellent understanding of AI concepts! Explore our advanced materials on AI agents and the future of AI to stay at the cutting edge.

questions.text: How often do you currently use AI tools (ChatGPT, Claude, Copilot, or similar) in your teaching practice?

options.text: Never or almost never
Occasionally — a few times per month
Regularly — at least weekly for lesson preparation or classroom activities

questions.text: What is 'prompt engineering' in the context of working with AI?

options.text: Programming AI systems using code
The skill of formulating clear, specific instructions to get better AI outputs
A technical process done by AI developers, not teachers
I'm not sure what this means

questions.text: You want to use AI to create a worksheet for your 6th-grade science class on photosynthesis. Which approach would give better results?

options.text: Type 'make a worksheet about photosynthesis'
Type 'Create a worksheet for 6th graders on photosynthesis, including 3 diagram-labeling tasks and 5 multiple-choice questions at basic comprehension level, suitable for a 20-minute activity'
I wouldn't know how to start

questions.text: What is 'context' in the context of AI conversation, and why does it matter?

options.text: It's the background information you give AI so it can generate more relevant responses
It's a technical setting I wouldn't need to worry about
It refers to the conversation history that AI uses as working memory
Both A and C are correct

questions.text: Which AI-powered educational tools have you used? (This helps us understand your experience breadth)

options.text: None yet
I've tried 1-2 tools (e.g., ChatGPT or Canva AI)
I regularly use 3+ different AI tools for different teaching tasks

feedback.beginner: You're just getting started with AI tools — that's perfectly fine! Our practical guides will walk you through using ChatGPT, Claude, and education-specific AI tools step by step.
feedback.intermediate: You've started using AI tools, which is great. Now it's time to refine your prompting skills and explore more specialized educational AI platforms.
feedback.advanced: You're an experienced AI tool user! Explore our advanced lessons on meta-prompting and building custom tools to take your practice further.

questions.text: How confident are you in discussing the ethical implications of using AI in education with colleagues or students?

options.text: Not confident — I haven't thought much about this
Somewhat confident — I'm aware of some issues like privacy and bias
Very confident — I can discuss bias, data privacy, academic integrity, and pedagogical implications

questions.text: A student submits an essay that you suspect was entirely written by AI. What is your approach?

options.text: Use an AI detection tool and confront the student with the result
Focus on the learning process — have a conversation about the work and ask the student to explain their reasoning
I'm not sure how I would handle this situation

questions.text: Why is it important to review AI-generated educational materials before using them in class?

options.text: AI might generate content that is factually incorrect, biased, or inappropriate for the age group
It's a legal requirement
AI-generated materials are usually fine and don't need much review
I haven't thought about this

questions.text: What does 'human in the loop' mean in the context of AI in education?

options.text: A human must always be present when AI is running
Teachers maintain oversight and final decision-making authority over AI-generated outputs and recommendations
Students must always work in groups when using AI
I'm not familiar with this term

questions.text: Your school is considering adopting an AI-powered adaptive learning platform. What should be evaluated first?

options.text: The cost of the platform
Data privacy compliance (GDPR), what student data is collected, how it's stored, and pedagogical alignment with curriculum
Whether students will enjoy using it
I wouldn't know what to evaluate

feedback.beginner: Ethical and responsible use of AI is crucial. Start with our foundational materials on AI ethics, privacy, and the teacher's role in AI-assisted learning.
feedback.intermediate: You're aware of key issues. Deepen your understanding of GDPR compliance, academic integrity strategies, and how to develop school-level AI policies.
feedback.advanced: Strong ethical awareness! Explore our advanced materials on AI policy development and leading AI integration at the school level.

questions.text: How familiar are you with using technology (including AI) to support students with disabilities or special educational needs?

options.text: Not familiar — I haven't explored this area
Somewhat familiar — I know some tools exist but haven't used many
Very familiar — I regularly use assistive technology and/or AI tools for differentiated instruction

questions.text: Which of these is an example of AI being used to support a student with a visual impairment?

options.text: Using ChatGPT's voice mode to describe images or read text aloud
Using a spell-checker
Using a digital whiteboard
I'm not sure

questions.text: You have a student who recently arrived from another country and speaks very little of the language of instruction. How could AI help?

options.text: AI can't really help with language barriers
Use AI translation tools and text-to-speech to provide materials in the student's native language while they learn
Use AI to simplify and adapt text complexity, provide bilingual glossaries, and create visual supports alongside translated materials

questions.text: What is Microsoft Immersive Reader and how can it support learners?

options.text: A paid Microsoft Office add-on for creating presentations
A free tool that reads text aloud, breaks words into syllables, highlights parts of speech, and translates — supporting students with dyslexia, visual impairments, or language barriers
A screen magnifier for low-vision users
I haven't heard of it

questions.text: How would you rate your ability to adapt teaching materials for students with different learning needs using AI tools?

options.text: I wouldn't know where to start
I could do basic adaptations (e.g., simplifying text) but not much more
I can use multiple AI tools to differentiate materials across visual, auditory, cognitive, and language needs

feedback.beginner: AI offers powerful tools for supporting diverse learners. Start with our overview of accessibility tools and learn how AI can help students with different needs.
feedback.intermediate: You have some awareness of accessibility tools. Explore our specific guides on supporting students with visual, hearing, cognitive, and language needs.
feedback.advanced: Great expertise in accessibility! Explore our advanced materials on building comprehensive inclusive classrooms with AI and our tools directory.
